content:
# Luke Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 115-117 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_022_page_001.jpg, LUK_022_page_002.jpg, LUK_022_page_003.jpg

---

### The Plot Against Jesus (vv. 1-6)

**1** The Festival of Unleavened Bread — called the Passover — was approaching. **2** The chief priests and the scribes were looking for a way to do away with Jesus, but they were afraid of the people.

**3** Then Satan entered Judas, the one called Iscariot, who was one of the Twelve. **4** He went and discussed with the chief priests and the temple guard officers how he might hand Jesus over to them. **5** They were delighted and agreed to pay him money. **6** He accepted, and from that point on he watched for an opportunity to betray Jesus when no crowd was around.

### Preparing the Passover (vv. 7-13)

**7** Then came the day of Unleavened Bread, on which the Passover lamb had to be sacrificed. **8** Jesus sent Peter and John, saying, "Go and prepare the Passover meal for us, so we can eat it together."

**9** "Where do you want us to prepare it?" they asked.

**10** He told them, "When you enter the city, a man carrying a water jar will meet you. Follow him into the house he enters, **11** and say to the owner of the house, 'The Teacher asks you: Where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?' **12** He will show you a large upstairs room, already furnished and ready. Make the preparations there." **13** They went and found everything just as he had told them, and they prepared the Passover.

### The Last Supper (vv. 14-23)

**14** When the hour came, he reclined at the table, and the apostles with him. **15** He said to them, "I have longed intensely to eat this Passover with you before I suffer. **16** For I tell you, I will not eat it again until it finds its fulfillment in the kingdom of God."

**17** Then he took a cup, gave thanks, and said, "Take this and share it among yourselves. **18** For I tell you, from now on I will not drink the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes."

**19** Then he took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them, saying, "This is my body, given for you. Do this to remember me." **20** In the same way, after the meal he took the cup, saying, "This cup is the new covenant sealed in my blood, which is poured out for you.

**21** "But look — the hand of my betrayer is here on this table with mine. **22** The Son of Man is going to his fate, as it has been determined. But how terrible it will be for the man who betrays him!" **23** They began to question one another about which of them could possibly do such a thing.

### The Dispute About Greatness (vv. 24-30)

**24** A dispute also broke out among them over which of them should be considered the greatest. **25** Jesus said to them, "The kings of the nations lord it over their subjects, and those in authority over them call themselves 'Benefactors.' **26** But you must not be like that. Instead, the greatest among you must become like the youngest, and the leader must become like the one who serves. **27** For who is greater — the one at the table or the one serving? Is it not the one at the table? Yet here I am among you as the one who serves.

**28** "You are the ones who have stayed with me through my trials. **29** And just as my Father has granted me a kingdom, I am granting you the same — **30** so that you will eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, and you will sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel."

### Peter's Denial Predicted (vv. 31-34)

**31** "Simon, Simon — listen! Satan has demanded to sift all of you like wheat. **32** But I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith will not collapse. And when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers."

**33** Peter said to him, "Lord, I am ready to go with you to prison and to death!"

**34** Jesus replied, "I tell you, Peter — the rooster will not crow today before you have denied three times that you know me."

### The Two Swords (vv. 35-38)

**35** Then he asked them, "When I sent you out without a money bag, a traveler's pack, or sandals — did you lack anything?"

"Nothing," they replied.

**36** He said to them, "But now, if you have a money bag, take it — and a traveler's pack as well. And if you don't have a sword, sell your cloak and buy one. **37** For I tell you, what has been written must be fulfilled in me: 'He was counted among the outlaws.' Indeed, what is written about me is reaching its conclusion."

**38** They said, "Lord, look — here are two swords."

He replied, "That is enough."

### Prayer on the Mount of Olives (vv. 39-46)

**39** He went out and made his way to the Mount of Olives as was his custom, and his disciples followed him. **40** When he arrived at the place, he said to them, "Pray that you will not fall into temptation."

**41** He withdrew from them about a stone's throw, knelt down, and began to pray. **42** "Father, if you are willing, take this cup away from me. Yet not my will, but yours be done."

**43** An angel from heaven appeared to him and strengthened him. **44** In anguish, he prayed more intensely, and his sweat fell to the ground like drops of blood.

**45** When he rose from prayer and went back to the disciples, he found them asleep, exhausted by grief. **46** "Why are you sleeping?" he asked them. "Get up and pray, so that you will not fall into temptation."

### The Arrest (vv. 47-53)

**47** While he was still speaking, a crowd appeared. The man called Judas — one of the Twelve — was leading them. He approached Jesus to kiss him, **48** but Jesus said to him, "Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?"

**49** When those around Jesus saw what was about to happen, they said, "Lord, should we strike with the sword?" **50** And one of them struck the high priest's servant and cut off his right ear. **51** But Jesus responded, "Stop! No more of this!" And he touched the man's ear and healed him.

**52** Then Jesus said to the chief priests, temple guard officers, and elders who had come for him, "Am I some kind of rebel, that you come after me with swords and clubs? **53** Day after day I was with you in the temple, and you never laid a hand on me. But this is your hour — the hour when darkness rules."

### Peter's Denial (vv. 54-62)

**54** They seized Jesus and led him away, bringing him to the high priest's house. Peter followed at a distance. **55** When they had lit a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sat down together, Peter sat down among them. **56** A servant girl saw him sitting by the firelight, stared at him, and said, "This man was with him too."

**57** But Peter denied it. "Woman, I don't know him," he said.

**58** A little later, someone else saw him and said, "You are one of them too."

"I am not," Peter replied.

**59** About an hour later, another person insisted, "Certainly this man was with him — he is a Galilean!"

**60** Peter said, "I don't know what you're talking about!" And immediately, while he was still speaking, a rooster crowed. **61** The Lord turned and looked straight at Peter. Then Peter remembered the Lord's words — how he had said to him, "Before the rooster crows today, you will deny me three times." **62** And he went outside and wept bitterly.

### Jesus Mocked and Beaten (vv. 63-65)

**63** The men guarding Jesus began mocking and beating him. **64** They blindfolded him and demanded, "Prophesy! Who hit you?" **65** And they hurled all kinds of insults at him.

### Jesus Before the Council (vv. 66-71)

**66** When daylight came, the council of the elders of the people assembled — both chief priests and scribes — and they brought him before their Sanhedrin. **67** "If you are the Messiah," they said, "tell us."

He replied, "If I tell you, you will not believe me. **68** And if I ask you a question, you will not answer. **69** But from now on, the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God."

**70** They all said, "So you are the Son of God, then?"

He answered them, "You are the ones saying that I am."

**71** They said, "What further testimony do we need? We have heard it ourselves from his own mouth!"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Plot and Judas (vv. 1-6)

Luke's account is distinctive in attributing Judas's betrayal directly to Satanic agency: EISELTHEN DE SATANAS EIS IOUDAN ("Satan entered into Judas"). This is not merely a motivation or temptation — Luke presents it as a kind of possession. The cosmic conflict introduced in Luke 4:13 (where Satan departed from Jesus "until an opportune time," ACHRI KAIROU) now reaches its climax. Judas seeks to hand Jesus over ATER OCHLOU ("apart from a crowd"), because the authorities feared the people's reaction. I rendered the Greek STRATEGOS TOU HIEROU as "temple guard officers" — these were the Levitical police force responsible for order in the temple precincts, second in authority only to the high priest.

### The Last Supper — Cup-Bread-Cup Sequence (vv. 14-23)

Luke's account is unique among the Synoptics in presenting a cup-bread-cup sequence (vv. 17-20), which corresponds to the structure of the Passover seder with its multiple cups of wine. Verse 15 contains EPITHYMIA EPETHYMESA — a Hebraism using a cognate noun with the verb ("with desire I have desired"), which I rendered as "I have longed intensely" to capture the emotional force. The word EPITHUMIA here is not negative desire (as in Paul's usage) but deep, passionate yearning.

The words of institution in vv. 19-20 are present in Sinaiticus. Some scholars have debated whether these verses belong to the original text of Luke, but they are clearly present in our manuscript. The phrase HE KAINE DIATHEKE EN TO HAIMATI MOU ("the new covenant in my blood") echoes Jeremiah 31:31-34. I translated DIATHEKE as "covenant" rather than "testament" because the context is sacrificial and relational, not legal. The addition "sealed in my blood" for EN TO HAIMATI MOU emphasizes that blood is the ratifying agent of the covenant — a dynamic rendering of the instrumental dative.

### The Dispute About Greatness (vv. 24-30)

The placement of this dispute — immediately after the Supper and the betrayal announcement — is devastating in its irony. While Jesus speaks of his body broken and blood poured out, the disciples argue about rank. The title EUERGETAI ("Benefactors") was an official title used by Hellenistic rulers (e.g., Ptolemy III Euergetes). Jesus subverts political vocabulary: the word DIAKONON ("the one who serves") is the root of our word "deacon" and described the lowest household servant. I rendered v. 27 with rhetorical punch to match the Greek contrast between HO ANAKEIMENOS (the one reclining at table) and HO DIAKONON (the one serving).

### Satan's Demand and Peter's Fall (vv. 31-34)

The verb EXETESATO ("has demanded") in v. 31 is striking — Satan has formally requested permission to test the disciples, echoing the opening of Job. The image of sifting wheat (SINISAI) involves violent shaking to separate grain from chaff. Note the pronoun shift: "all of you" (HYMAS, plural) are demanded for sifting, but "I have prayed for you" (SOU, singular) — Jesus' intercessory prayer is directed specifically at Peter. The phrase EPISTREPAS STERISON ("when you have turned back, strengthen") presupposes both the fall and the restoration. Peter's boast of readiness for prison and death (v. 33) makes his subsequent triple denial all the more devastating.

### The Two Swords (vv. 35-38)

This is one of Luke's most debated passages. Jesus quotes Isaiah 53:12 (META ANOMON ELOGISTHE, "he was counted among the lawless") and tells them to buy swords. The disciples produce two literal swords; Jesus says HIKANON ESTIN ("it is enough" or "that is sufficient"). Most scholars read this as Jesus cutting short a misunderstanding — the disciples have taken literally what was meant to signal that a time of crisis and opposition has arrived. I rendered it as "That is enough" with its natural double meaning: enough swords (from the disciples' perspective) and enough of this conversation (from Jesus' perspective).

### The Agony in Gethsemane and the Textual Variant (vv. 39-46)

**Critical Textual Note on vv. 43-44:** These two verses — describing an angel strengthening Jesus and his sweat becoming like drops of blood — represent one of the most significant textual variants in Luke's Gospel. In Codex Sinaiticus, the situation is complex: the original scribe (Scribe A) appears to have included these verses, though there is evidence of later correction activity on this page. The verses are absent from P75 and Codex Vaticanus (B), two of the earliest and most important Alexandrian witnesses, as well as from several early manuscripts (A, N, T, W, family 13, and others). They are present in Sinaiticus (original hand), Codex Bezae (D), the Old Latin, and the majority of later manuscripts.

The scholarly debate centers on whether these verses were original to Luke and later removed (perhaps because the portrayal of Jesus in extreme distress was seen as theologically problematic) or whether they were a later addition from early tradition (perhaps originally a marginal note drawn from oral tradition that was incorporated into the text). The medical detail of HAIMATOS (blood-like sweat, a condition known as hematidrosis) and the appearance of an angel are consistent with Lukan themes (angels appear frequently in Luke-Acts), which could support either scenario.

**PNEUMA's decision:** I have included vv. 43-44 in the translation text because they are present in Codex Sinaiticus, which is our governing manuscript. However, translators and readers should be aware of their disputed status. Their dramatic power is undeniable: they present the most viscerally human portrait of Jesus in the Passion narrative.

The word AGONIA (v. 44) does not mean simply "agony" in the modern sense of pain — in Greek it denotes the intense anxiety and inner struggle of an athlete before a contest. I rendered it as "anguish" to capture the internal dimension. EKTENESTERON ("more intensely") in the comparative degree suggests prayer that is stretched taut, strained to its limit.

### The Arrest and the Ear (vv. 47-53)

Only Luke records Jesus healing the severed ear (v. 51). The word HAPSOMENOS ("having touched") is characteristic of Luke's interest in Jesus' physical healing touch. Jesus' final words to his captors are uniquely Lukan: HAUTE ESTIN HYMON HE HORA KAI HE EXOUSIA TOU SKOTOUS — "this is your hour and the authority of the darkness." The word EXOUSIA ("authority, power") applied to darkness personifies it as a ruling force — this is Satan's counter-hour, permitted but bounded.

### Peter's Denial and the Look (vv. 54-62)

Only Luke records that "the Lord turned and looked at Peter" (v. 61). The verb ENEBLEPSEN means not a casual glance but a direct, penetrating gaze. This single look carries the weight of the entire relationship — prediction, love, grief, and the implicit call to repentance. Peter's response, EKLAUSEN PIKROS ("he wept bitterly"), uses the adverb of sharp, piercing pain. The bitterness is not just sorrow but the taste of his own failure.

### The Trial Before the Council (vv. 66-71)

Luke compresses the trial into a single morning session (unlike Mark's night trial followed by a morning confirmation). The Sanhedrin's question comes in two stages: first about messiahship (v. 67), then about divine sonship (v. 70). Jesus' response to the first is a refusal to engage on their terms — they have already decided not to believe. His response to the second, HYMEIS LEGETE HOTI EGO EIMI ("You are the ones saying that I am"), is deliberately ambiguous in Greek. It neither fully affirms nor denies — it throws the declaration back onto the questioners. I translated it as "You are the ones saying that I am" to preserve this remarkable rhetorical move, where Jesus makes his accusers voice the very claim they seek to condemn.

The council's conclusion — TI ETI ECHOMEN MARTYRIAS CHREIAN ("What further need of testimony do we have?") — is bitterly ironic. They treat Jesus' words as self-incrimination, but the reader knows that Jesus has spoken the truth. The word from his own STOMA ("mouth") is simultaneously their legal evidence and the Gospel's proclamation.

---

*Translated from Codex Sinaiticus by PNEUMA*
*Luke Chapter 22 — 71 verses — The Passion begins*
